State of California
A-79-056

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Enforcement Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5600        322-5601        322-6441        322-6444

November 8, 1979


James P. Botz
County Counsel
County Administration Center
2555 Mendocino Avenue
Santa Rosa, CA 95401

Dear Mr. Botz:

Thank you for your letter dated August 3, 1979, and
follow-up correspondence of November 2, 1979, requesting an
informal opinion regarding the Conflict of Interest provision
of the Political Reform Act. I apologize for the lateness
of my response. I hope that the following comments are
helpful. The facts as I understand them may be stated as
follows.

The Sonoma County Board of Building Appeals was
established by the County pursuant to Section 7-4 of the
Sonoma County Code. The responsibility of the board is to
hear appeals from interested persons regarding the granting
or denial of building permits. The first question you have
asked our office to consider is whether the Board is required
to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the Act.
Second, you have asked whether the County may satisfy the
disclosure requirements of the Act by adopting a Code which
provides for transactional rather than periodic disclosure.
Finally, you have asked whether the provisions of Section 7-
4 of the Sonoma County Code meet the requirements of 2 Cal.
Admin. Code Section 18703(e).

Your analysis is correct that the Board of Building
Appeals is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code
pursuant to Government Code Section 87300, et seq. The Act
requires every agency to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code
and "agency" is defined to include any department, division,
bureau, office, board or commission of a county. See Government
Code Sections 82003 and 82041.

1/    A code reviewing body may exempt an agency from the
requirement to adopt a code if the agency will soon be nonfunctioning
or if it would have no designated employees within the meaning
of Government Code Section 82019. Enclosed for your information
is a copy of a memo to local code reviewing bodies from Ted
Prim setting forth in detail this exemption policy.

James P. Botz
November 4, 1979
Page Two


Your second question is whether, instead of requiring
annual periodic disclosure, the Board may place a provision
in its Conflict of Interest Code that would require financial
disclosure only if there is a governmental decision pending
before the Board affecting the interest. Such a provision
would not meet the requirements of Government Code Section
87302, which specifically states that:

[E]ach conflict of interest code shall contain the
following provisions: ... (b) requirements that
each designated employee file annual statements
disclosing reportable investments, interests in
real property and income.

[Emphasis added.]

The final question you posed is whether the provisions
of Section 7-4 of the Sonoma County Code meet the requirements
of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703, which provides:

A material financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official's interests, as described
in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d),
is distinguishable from all effect on the public
generally unless the decision will affect the
official's interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect all members of the public
or a significant segment of the public. Except as
provided herein, an industry, trade or profession
does not constitute a significant segment of the
general public.

...

(c) An industry, trade or profession constitutes
a significant segment of the public if the statute,
ordinance or other provision of law which creates
or authorizes the creation of the official's agency
or office contains a finding and declaration,
including an express reference to Section 87103 of
the Government Code, to the following effect:

The Legislature [or other authority] declares
that the individual[s] appointed to the office
of _____________ is [are] intended to represent
and further the interest of the [specified
industry, trade or profession], and that such
representation and furtherance will ultimately

James P. Botz
November 8, 1979
Page Three

serve the public interest. Accordingly, the
Legislature [or other authority] finds that
for purposes of persons who hold such office in
the [specified industry, trade or profession]
is tantamount to and constitutes the public
generally within the meaning of Section 87103
of the Government Code.

...

This provision will apply only if it was the intention
of the l gislative body, in setting up the agency to provide
for industry membership in the agency as a means to further
and represent the interests of the industry. A review of
Section 7-4 credibly the Board Appeals does seem to
support the conclusion that this was the intent of the Board
of Supervisors. Section 7-4 requires that Board members be
qualified by "experience and training to pass upon matters
pertaining to building construction." In addition, it is
stated in the section that it shall be the policy of the
Board to appoint at least one [specified city engineer,
licensed architect and contractor] but that the policy shall
in no way deprive the Board of its full discretion to appoint
otherwise qualified persons. It is not explicitly stated in
Section 7-4 that the Board members in one of the purposes
of representing and furthering the interests of particular
industries, and by giving the Board total discretion to
appoint an individual who does not fill any of those positions,
the section implies that the various professions mentioned
are to have membership on the Board for expertise purposes,
not to further the identified industries.

I have reviewed the proposed disclosure categories for
the Board of Appeals Conflict of Interest Code that your
office has developed. Disclosure categories 1, 2, and 3 are
designed to require Board members to disclose all investments,
interests in real property and sources of income. Based on
the responsibilities of the Board of Appeals, these disclosure
categories seem to be appropriate. I should note, however,
that category 4, which requires disclosure of interests in
business entities that may contract with the Board, is redundant.
This category is not necessary because all investments,
interests in real property and sources of income are required
to be disclosed. This includes those interests that are of
the type which contract with the Board.

There are several revisions which should be made to the
body of he Code. Section 20U, the disqualification provision
should be expanded to require disqualification by the public

James P. Botz
November 9, 1979
Page Four

official if he has a financial interest within the meaning
of Government Code Section 3710j, even if the interest is
not reportable. Section 500 should indicate that the county
will be providing disclosure forms and that assuming office
statements are required to be filed within 10 days after
assuming office. Section 600 should be revised so that
customers or clients of a business entity are disclosable
only if they fall within a designated employee's income
disclosure category. In addition, if we were reviewing to this
Code, we would remove the disclosure forms in order to avoid
frequent amendments to the Code because of changing or out-
dated disclosure forms.

Enclosed for your review is the Commission's new model
Conflict of Interest Code. You might want to have the Board
adopt a Code similar to this one in order to have a Code
that reflects current amendments to the Act, Commission
regulations and policies.

I hope this informal advice is helpful. If our office
may be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

Sincerely,

Dwight P. Dickerson
Counsel
Legal Division

JAMES P. BOTZ

JAMES P. BOTZ

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California

Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

     On August 3, 1979, and on September 17, 1979,
we requested your informal opinion on specific
questions, a copy of which we are attaching.

     To date we have had no reply. We would greatly
appreciate your reply at your very earliest
convenience.

                                        Yours truly,


                                        JAMES P. BOTZ
                                        County Counsel

JPB/k
Enclosure

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

Your informal opinion is requested on the following
questions:

1. Is the Sonoma County Board of Building
Appeals required to adopt a Conflict of
Interest Code?

2. Can the Board satisfy the requirements
of the Act by adopting a code that
requires transactional disclosure rather
than periodic disclosure?

I have enclosed for your benefit the ordinance
describing the qualifications of members of the Board
of Building Appeals and their duties and the permit
jurisdiction of the Chief Building Inspector whose decisions
are appealable to the Board.

I have determined, based on that information, that the
members of the Board of Building Appeals fall within the
requirements of § 87302(a) in that they are involved in the
making or participation in the making of decisions which
may foreseeably have a material effect on a financial
interest and thus must adopt a code. Further, my reading
of § 87302(b) is that the code must require annual, rather
than transactional, disclosure.

Some members of the Board of Building Appeals have
questioned this interpretation and along with a member of
the Board of Supervisors have requested that I obtain your
advice on the matter.

I have also enclosed a copy of the code prepared
for the Board for the purpose of inviting your comments.

We're truly yours,

JAMES P. BOTZ
Country Counsel

S.E.4

SAXAMANDAL DWAR

S.E.4

A Princes on the coromandel coast in the seventeenth century

The Dutch factory was established at this place. After sundry competitions
of Spanish corsairs and the records of the period dated from 1668 to 1752
are preserved in the archives at Batavia. The records shed much light on the
early history of the European intercourse with the south Indian coast. The
place was also visited by the British. A brief account is given in the MS
records of sea. E.I. Co. The Dutch records are preserved in the Algemeen
Rijksarchief at the Hague. Dr Moreland in his article on the Dutch at
Chinsura, mentions Saxamandal as one of the early Dutch settlements. The
place was also a centre of spice trade and was an important centre for the
sale of cotton goods from the Coromandel coast. The records of the VOC show
that there were considerable activities at this place. It was administered by
a Director of the factory and had a garrison. The factories of the European
companies were centers of political power and influence and were fortified
with cannons and armed guard. They served as distribution depots for the goods
of Europe and for the procurement of Indian commodities. B[illegible] ports of
call for merchant ships. The Dutch were not so successful as the British in
the control of sea. E.I. Co. But they maintained their trade with the east
Indies and were able to operate from their factories on the Coromandel coast
until the advent of French rule followed by the domination of the British.
The records at Batavia and at the Hague show that Saxamandal was an important
factory site for many years.

See p.431

Saxe.-.-

COUNTY OF SONOMA

JOHN C GAFFNEY
RICHARD E. DAFLER

COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
2300 MENDOCINO AVENUE

DEPUTIES
RICHARD W. ERGO
PRENTICE A. FISH
WILLIAM H. HAHN
CAROLINE A. KERL
MARK J. FREED

SANTA ROSA, CALIFORNIA 95401
TELEPHONE (707) 527-2421

August 3, 1979

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

Your informal opinion is requested on the following
questions:

1. Is the Sonoma County Board of Building
Appeals required to adopt a Conflict of
Interest Code?

2. Can the Board satisfy the requirements
of the Act by adopting a code that
requires transactional disclosure rather
than periodic disclosure?

3. Do the provisions of § 7-4 of the Sonoma
County Code (see materials enclosed) meet
the requirements of California Administrative Code § 1870(c)?

I have enclosed for your benefit the ordinance
describing the qualifications of members of the Board
of Building Appeals and their duties and the permit
jurisdiction of the Chief Building Inspector whose decisions
are appealable to the Board.

I have determined, based on that information, that the
members of the Board of Building Appeals fall within the
requirements of Government Code § 87302(a) in that they are
involved in the making or participation in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on
a financial interest and thus must adopt a code. Further,
my reading of Gov. Code § 87302(b) is that the code must
require annual, rather than transactional, disclosure.

Some members of the Board of Building Appeals have
questioned this interpretation and along with a member
of the Board of Supervisors have requested that I obtain
your advice on the matter.

Fair Political Practices Commission

-2- August 3, 1979

I have also enclosed a copy of the code prepared
for the Board for the purposes of inviting your comments.
I am particularly interested in narrowing the scope of
disclosure wherever possible.

Yours truly,

[signature]
JAMES P. BOTZ
County Counsel

JPB/k

Enclosures

CC: Leon Anderson, Deputy Director
    Building Inspection, County of Sonoma

§ 7-6                    SONOMA COUNTY CODE                    § 7-6

(c) Exemptions. The provisiona of this section shall not ap-
ply to any building designed and constructed primarily for
use in housing livestock, poultry, hay, grain or farm ma-
chinery or supplies located on a parcel of land that is currently
primarily used for agriculture; provided, that this exemption
shall not apply to the following:

(1) Any building which requires the presence of
workers, customers or assemblages of any kind within such
building in order to carry out any purpose for which the build-
ing is designed.

(2) Any structure situated upon a parcel of property of
three acres or less in one ownership.

(3) Any structure, any part of which is within fifty feet
of a property line, regardless of the size of the parcel.

(d) Application for exemption. Prior to the erection, con-
struction, conversion, enlargement or major alteration of any
building or structure situated on agricultural land or prior to
the moving of any building or structure onto such land, a
written application for the exemption shall be filed with the
chief building inspector and shall contain:

(1) A verified statement in writing signed by the owner
of the agricultural land recording each of the requirements
contained in subsection (c);

(2) Description of the present and primary use of the
land;

(3) Description of the building or structure to be
exempted;

(4) Name and address of applicant and of the owner of
the land;

(5) A precise plot plan showing all buildings, structures,
property lines, streets and roads within one hundred feet of
the proposed building or structure and the means of access
thereto;

(6) Such further information as the chief building in-
spector may require.

(e) Fees for exempt work. There will be no fee; provided,
that the exemption is secured before work is started. In the

164

Supp. 9-74

§ 7-6                    BUILDING REGULATIONS                    § 7-6

cleared as to zoning considerations by the county planning de-
partment and as to drainage and flood control clearance where
applicable by the county water agency prior to their issuance.
Further, where road encroachment is necessary, a permit for
same shall be first secured from the county road department,
a division of the department of public works. A sanitarian de-
partment clearance is first required in areas served by
sanitation districts. Building permits must be cleared as to
agricultural preserve regulations and compliance with the
Williamson Act by the Agricultural Preserve Advisory Committee,
or designated representative of such other agency as may be
specified by resolution of this Board of Supervisors.

Notwithstanding any other provision of this chapter or
the codes adopted hereby, maintenance work or repair of
buildings and structures requiring a permit hereunder may be
commenced before obtaining a permit without violating this
chapter; provided, that the building inspection department or
the public health officer, in the appropriate case is notified
prior to noon of the next following business day and the per-
mit is obtained is obtained within twenty-four hours thereafter;
and provided further, that no work shall be covered before it
has been duly inspected and approved. Compliance with chap-
ter 26, the Subdivision Map Act, the county Subdivision Ordi-
nance, the Williamson Act, agricultural preserve regulations and
any Williamson Act easement or contract applying to the subject
property, and the County Zoning Ordinance, including
compliance with conditional permits issued thereunder, is a
condition precedent to the issuance of any permit required by
this chapter for work to be done on any particular parcel of
real property in the unincorporated area of this county. (Ord.
No. 1851, 5-1)

(b) Performance bonds for moved buildings. A perfor-
mance bond or a cash deposit equal to the amount of the bond
is required to be posted prior to the issuance of a permit to
move and rehabilitate a building. Such bond or deposit must
be in the amount of all costs for moving and rehabilitation in
meeting current regulations, as determined by the chief build-
ing inspector.

163                                                                           Supp. 9-74

§ 76

HEMORRHOID ATLAS

§ 76

To the extent internal hemorrhoid pictures provided constitute art work protected by copyright, said pictures are provided to you under a limited license for personal, non-commercial viewing only. The pictures, artwork and associated text may not be reproduced, distributed, transmitted, cached, or otherwise used, except in connection with the permitted personal, non-commercial use, without the prior express written consent of the copyright holder. Except as permitted by law, modification of the pictures, artwork or text, or use of them for any other purpose, is prohibited. Title and ownership, and all rights with respect to the content remain in the copyright holder. These restrictions apply to all content on this page.

MC 1

Source p 24

[Small circles appear at the top and left margins of the page]

S 23

SPECIA.L APPENDIX

S 24

IF INDEX REGULATIONS

Sec. 25. Special appendix.

(a) In addition to the information required to be disclosed under sections 11 through 24, the issuer shall disclose in a special appendix:

(1) Any material changes in the issuer's capital structure since the date of the last financial statements included in the prospectus or registration statement;

(2) Any material subsequent events;

(3) The names and titles of the principal executive officers;

(4) The names and titles of the members of the board of directors;

(5) A description of any underwriting arrangements or selling groups;

(6) The names and addresses of the principal underwriters;

(7) A statement of the amount of the underwriters' compensation;

(8) The general character and location of the principal properties of the issuer;

(9) Any material litigation pending or threatened against the issuer;

(10) Any material relationships or transactions with related parties;

(11) A description of any material intellectual property rights held by the issuer;

(12) Any material environmental matters affecting the issuer;

(13) A description of the issuer's compliance with applicable laws and regulations;

(14) Any material industry-specific information;

(15) A description of the issuer's risk factors;

(16) Any material pending or threatened governmental proceedings;

(17) Any material contracts not made in the ordinary course of business;

(18) Any material acquisition or disposition of assets;

(19) Any material changes in the issuer's business or operations;

(20) Any material changes in the market for the issuer's products or services;

(21) Any material changes in the issuer's competitive position;

(22) Any material changes in the issuer's management or board of directors;

(23) Any material changes in the issuer's accounting policies or practices;

(24) Any material off-balance sheet arrangements or obligations;

(25) Any material contingent liabilities;

(26) Any material restrictions on the transferability of securities;

(27) Any material anti-dilution provisions;

(28) Any material conversion or redemption rights;

(29) Any material dividend restrictions or preferences;

(30) Any material preemptive rights;

(31) Any material information regarding the issuer's tax status;

(32) Any material regulatory matters affecting the issuer;

(33) Any material insurance policies;

(34) Any material changes in the issuer's financial condition;

(35) Any material changes in the issuer's results of operations;

(36) Any material cash flow changes;

(37) Any material changes in working capital;

(38) Any material credit facilities or financing arrangements;

(39) Any material commitments or obligations;

(40) Any material off-balance sheet financing arrangements;

(41) Any material guarantees or indemnifications;

(42) Any material lease arrangements;

(43) Any material pension or other post-employment benefit obligations;

(44) Any material stock-based compensation arrangements;

(45) Any material derivative financial instruments;

(46) Any material hedging activities;

(47) Any material fair value measurements;

(48) Any material segment information;

(49) Any material revenue recognition policies;

(50) Any material accounting estimates;

(51) Any material changes in estimates;

(52) Any material material weakness in internal controls;

(53) Any material significant deficiencies in internal controls;

(54) Any material going concern issues;

(55) Any material subsequent events through the date the registration statement was filed;

Source ___

Source
____
S 24

(b) The special appendix shall be clearly labeled and shall include cross-references to the relevant sections of the prospectus or registration statement where additional information regarding each item may be found.

(c) The information disclosed in the special appendix shall be presented in a clear, concise, and understandable manner, using plain language and avoiding unnecessary technical jargon.

(d) The issuer shall ensure that all information disclosed in the special appendix is accurate, complete, and not misleading.

(e) The issuer shall update the special appendix to reflect any material changes or updates that occur after the initial filing but before the effective date of the registration statement.

(f) The special appendix shall be included as an exhibit to the registration statement or prospectus and shall be available to investors prior to purchase of the securities.

S 23

sections.question: 1. Is the Sonoma County Board of Building Appeals required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code? 2. Can the Board satisfy the requirements of the Act by adopting a code that requires transactional disclosure rather than periodic disclosure? 3. Whether the provisions of Section 7-4 of the Sonoma County Code meet the requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703(c).
sections.conclusion: Your analysis is correct that the Board of Building Appeals is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code Section 87300, et seq. Such a provision would not meet the requirements of Government Code Section 87302, which specifically requires annual statements disclosing reportable investments, interests in real property and income. A review of Section 7-4 creating the Board of Appeals does not seem to support the conclusion that Board members are appointed to represent particular industries, but rather for expertise purposes.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Sonoma County Board of Building Appeals must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code, whether it may use transactional rather than periodic disclosure, and whether existing county code provisions satisfy regulatory requirements for designating industries as representing the public interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Board must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code with annual periodic disclosure rather than transactional disclosure, and the existing Section 7-4 does not satisfy the regulatory exception allowing industry representation, but the FPPC finds the proposed disclosure categories 1-3 appropriate while noting category 4 is redundant.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter from County Counsel (dated November 2, 1979 follow-up to August 3, 1979 initial request) and FPPC response letter from Counsel Dwight E. Dickerson (dated November 8, 1979). Extracted information primarily from FPPC response section. Some text truncated at end of document. High confidence in core Q/C extraction from substantive FPPC opinion.

QUESTION: 1. Is the Sonoma County Board of Building Appeals required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code? 2. Can the Board satisfy the requirements of the Act by adopting a code that requires transactional disclosure rather than periodic disclosure? 3. Whether the provisions of Section 7-4 of the Sonoma County Code meet the requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703(c).

CONCLUSION: Your analysis is correct that the Board of Building Appeals is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code Section 87300, et seq. Such a provision would not meet the requirements of Government Code Section 87302, which specifically requires annual statements disclosing reportable investments, interests in real property and income. A review of Section 7-4 creating the Board of Appeals does not seem to support the conclusion that Board members are appointed to represent particular industries, but rather for expertise purposes.

I apologize for the lateness of my response. I hope that the following comments are helpful. The facts as I understand them may be stated as follows. The Sonoma County Board of Building Appeals was established by the County pursuant to Section 7-4 of the Sonoma County Code. The responsibility of the Board is to hear appeals from interested persons regarding the granting or denial of building permits. The first question you have asked our office to consider is whether the Board is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the Act. Second, you have asked whether the agency may satisfy the disclosure requirements of the Act by adopting a Code which provides for transactional rather than periodic disclosure. Finally, you have asked whether the provisions of Section 7-4 of the Sonoma County Code meet the requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703(c). Your analysis is correct that the Board of Building Appeals is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code Section 87300, et seq. Code Section 18703, which provides: A material financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's interests, as described in Government Code Section 87103(a) through (d), is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of the public or a significant
embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Sonoma County's request for guidance on whether the Board of Building Appeals must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code and whether it may use transactional rather than annual periodic disclosure. The FPPC confirms the requirement for a code with annual disclosure, addresses whether the board meets the industry representation exception, and provides detailed comments on the proposed disclosure categories and recommended revisions to the code.
